questions.question: What is a database in Notion?

options.explanation: Notion databases are built natively, not imported from Excel.

options.text: A collection of pages with structured properties
options.explanation: A Notion database is a collection of pages, each with customizable properties that let you organize and filter information.
Notion databases are not SQL databases; they use a proprietary system.
Folders don't exist in Notion; databases are structured collections with properties.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a database view type in Notion?

options.explanation: Table is one of the standard database views.
Board (Kanban) is a standard database view.
Notion does not have a Gantt view. It offers Table, Board, Timeline, Calendar, List, and Gallery views.
Gallery is a standard database view.

questions.question: What happens when you click on a row in a Notion database?

options.explanation: Clicking a row doesn't just edit a cell.

options.text: It opens the row as a full page
options.explanation: Every database row in Notion is a page. Clicking it opens that page where you can add any content.

options.text: It selects the row for bulk actions
options.explanation: You need to use the checkbox to select rows for bulk actions.

options.text: It starts editing the row title inline
options.explanation: While you can edit inline, clicking the row itself opens it as a page.

questions.question: Which property type would you use to link entries between two databases?

options.explanation: URL properties store web links, not database relations.
The Relation property type creates connections between entries in two databases.
Multi-select stores tags, not connections to other databases.
There is no 'Reference' property type in Notion.

questions.question: What is a rollup property in Notion?

options.text: A property that summarizes data from a related database
options.explanation: Rollups pull data through a Relation property and compute aggregations like sum, average, or count from the related entries.

options.text: A property that rolls back changes to a previous version
options.explanation: Rollups are not related to version history.

options.text: A property that combines multiple properties into one
options.explanation: That would be closer to a formula, not a rollup.

options.text: A property that automatically archives old entries
options.explanation: Rollups don't archive anything; they aggregate related data.

questions.question: How do you add a filter to a Notion database?

options.explanation: Right-clicking the title doesn't open filter options.

options.text: Click the Filter button in the database toolbar
options.explanation: The Filter button in the toolbar above the database lets you add filter rules based on property values.
Notion doesn't use SQL; filtering is done through the visual interface.
There is no /filter slash command. Filters are applied via the toolbar.

questions.question: What is the difference between a full-page and inline database?

options.text: Full-page databases can have more properties
options.explanation: Both types support the same properties.
Both types are fully editable.

options.text: A full-page database occupies its own page, while inline sits within another page
options.explanation: Full-page databases take up an entire page. Inline databases are embedded within the content of another page alongside other blocks.
Inline databases support all the same views as full-page ones.

questions.question: What is the highest permission level in Notion?

options.explanation: Editor can edit but doesn't have all administrative powers.
Full access is the highest permission level, allowing the user to edit, share, and manage permissions for the page.
Admin is a workspace-level role, not a page permission level.
Owner is not a permission level in Notion's sharing system.

questions.question: What does the 'Can view' permission allow?

options.text: View and comment on a page
options.explanation: 'Can view' only allows viewing. Commenting requires 'Can comment' permission.
'Can view' allows the user to read the content but not edit or comment.
Duplication may be restricted separately by the workspace admin.

options.text: View the page title but not the content
options.explanation: 'Can view' grants access to the full page content, not just the title.

questions.question: How do you share a Notion page with someone outside your workspace?

options.text: Export it as PDF and email it
options.explanation: Exporting creates a static file, not a shared Notion page.

options.text: Add them as a guest using their email address
options.explanation: You can invite people outside your workspace as guests by entering their email in the Share menu.

options.text: Move the page to their workspace
options.explanation: You can't move pages between workspaces this way.

options.text: You can only share within your workspace
options.explanation: Notion supports sharing with external guests.

questions.question: What does 'Publish to web' do?

options.text: Creates a standalone website from your Notion page
options.explanation: It creates a read-only public link, not a standalone website.

options.text: Makes the page publicly accessible via a link
options.explanation: 'Publish to web' generates a public URL that anyone with the link can view without needing a Notion account.

options.text: Uploads the page to a web hosting service
options.explanation: The page stays on Notion's servers; it just becomes publicly accessible.

options.text: Sends the page content to a blog platform
options.explanation: 'Publish to web' keeps the page on Notion, not on external platforms.

questions.question: What is the difference between a Member and a Guest in Notion?

options.text: Members can only view, guests can edit
options.explanation: It's the opposite in terms of default access scope.

options.text: Members belong to the workspace; guests are invited to specific pages
options.explanation: Members are part of the workspace and have access to shared workspace content. Guests are external users invited to specific pages only.
Members and guests have fundamentally different access scopes.

options.text: Guests have higher permissions than members
options.explanation: Guests typically have more restricted access than members.

questions.question: Can you restrict permission inheritance for child pages?

options.text: No, child pages always inherit parent permissions
options.explanation: You can customize permissions for child pages.

options.text: Yes, by removing inherited access in the page's Share settings
options.explanation: You can override inherited permissions by opening the Share menu on a child page and removing or changing access for specific users or groups.

options.text: Only workspace admins can do this
options.explanation: Anyone with full access to a page can modify its sharing settings.
Permission customization for child pages is available on all plans.

questions.question: What is a teamspace in Notion?

options.text: A separate Notion account for teams
options.explanation: Teamspaces exist within a single workspace, not as separate accounts.

options.text: A section within a workspace for organizing team content with its own permissions
options.explanation: Teamspaces let teams organize their pages within the workspace with customizable access levels (open, closed, or private).
Teamspaces are for content organization, not video calls.

options.text: A shared calendar for team events
options.explanation: Teamspaces organize pages and content, not calendars specifically.

questions.question: Which shortcut creates a new page in Notion?

options.explanation: Ctrl+N (Windows) or Cmd+N (Mac) creates a new page in the current workspace.
Ctrl+P opens Quick Find/Search, not a new page.
Ctrl+T is not a standard Notion shortcut.
Alt+N is not the shortcut for creating a new page.

questions.question: How do you open Notion's search/quick find?

options.explanation: Ctrl+F searches within the current page, not Notion-wide search.
Ctrl+P (or Cmd+P on Mac) opens Quick Find, which lets you search across all pages in your workspace.
Ctrl+K is used for creating links, not for search.
F3 is not a Notion shortcut.

questions.question: Which shortcut toggles bold formatting in Notion?

options.explanation: Ctrl+I toggles italic, not bold.
Ctrl+U toggles underline, not bold.
Ctrl+B (or Cmd+B on Mac) toggles bold formatting on selected text.
The standard bold shortcut doesn't include Shift.

questions.question: How do you create a to-do/checkbox block using a keyboard shortcut?

options.explanation: While /todo works as a slash command, the question asks about keyboard shortcuts.
Ctrl+Shift+4 (or Cmd+Shift+4) creates a to-do checkbox block.
The markdown shortcut is actually [] (brackets), but the keyboard shortcut is Ctrl+Shift+4.
Ctrl+T is not a shortcut for creating to-do blocks.

questions.question: What does Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + H do in Notion?

options.explanation: Page history is accessed through the menu, not this shortcut.

options.text: Toggles the last used text or highlight color
options.explanation: Ctrl+Shift+H applies the last used highlight color to selected text.
Hiding the sidebar uses a different shortcut.
This shortcut is for highlighting, not help.

questions.question: How do you indent a block in Notion?

options.explanation: While this works in some apps, Notion uses Tab for indenting.
Pressing Tab indents (nests) the current block under the block above it.
This moves the cursor, not the block.
Alt+Tab switches windows, not indents blocks.
